translation: From the bottom of the chest (heart).
From the beginning.
Deep calls unto deep. One misstep leads to another.
Drama has been acted out. These words announced the end of a performance in a Roman theatre.  Emperor August said these words on his deathbed.
To archives. Not actual any more.
To high places by narrow roads.
Exactly for that. Also: Not prearranged, informal.
In honour. Honour not bearing any material advantage.
To infinity.  Endlessly.
Freely. Without restraint, as desired.
To the point of being disgusted.

The dice have been cast. The decision has been made. Supposedly words of Julius Caesar as he was going to cross river Rubicon with his army on his way toward Rome.  For an army leader it was forbidden to cross this river with an army, so that moment was a start of civil war.
Another I. Soul mate, close friend.
A friend is another self.
A snake in the grass. A treacherous person.
The spirit makes (human) noble.
An eagle doesn't catch flies.
Art for art's sake. Art has its own sense.
Skill (of medicine) is long, life (of a patient and doctor) is short.
The donkey scratches the donkey. Stupid and conceited people flatter each other about qualities they do not possess.
Let us hear the opposite side!
I will either find a way or I will make one.
Hail, Caesar, those who are about to die salute you! According to Suatonius, words said by gladiators.
One who has lived well has lived unnoticed.
Blind are led by the blind. Leaders are not more knowledgeable than the ones they lead.
Seize the day.
The occasion of war. An event or action that (allegedly) justifies a war or conflict.
Let the buyer beware. Buyer must ensure that the goods about to be purchased are free from defects.
Let arms yield to the toga. Let violence give place to law.
Other things (being) equal. All variables other than the ones being studied are assumed to be constant.

latin: Ceterum censeo: delenda est Carthago. Ceterum censeo Carthaginem delendam esse.
translation: And in conclusion I think: Carthage ought to be destroyed. And in conclusion I think that Carthage ought to be destroyed. Closing words of every speech by Cato the Elder in the Roman Senate, regardless of the topic of the debate, in aim to keep the renewal of war against Carthage on the agenda.  Eventually, Carthage was, in fact, destroyed by the Romans.
Clear agreements, good friends.
I think, therefore I am.
A common shipwreck is sweet. Common traumas feel good and connect people.
Work together to accomplish more.
A condition, without which, not. An essential condition or requirement.
The body of a crime. The facts of a crime.
Believe that you have it, and you have it.

latin: Crudelius est quam mori semper timere mortem.
translation: It is crueller to be always afraid of dying than to die.
Patience is the cure for all suffering.
With a grain of salt. Take something not literally, but with due consideration.
With great success.
The run of life.
They (people) condemn what they do not understand.
In fact.
By law. According to law.
Tastes are not to be argued.
The law does not concern itself with trifles.
(Say) nothing but good about the dead.
The god from a device. Sudden, unexpected turnover. Locution from Greek dramas, in which often, at some point, gods appear to resolve an unresolvable situation in a sudden, unexpected way.
He who has begun has the job half done.
Part and rule. Roman maxima of ruling the subdued nations.
Give to obtain (something back).
Teach in order to learn. We learn by teaching.

latin: Dulce et decorum est pro patria mori.
translation: It is sweet and proper to die for one's country.
Through difficulty, sweetness.
As long as I breathe, I hope.
The law is hard, but it is law.

latin: Eram quod es, eris quod sum.
translation: I was what you are, you will be what I am.

latin: Errare humanum est, in errore perservare stultum.
translation: It is human to make a mistake, it is stupid to persist in it.
There is a middle course in all things.
You too, Brutus! Even you have betrayed me! The exclamation of Julius Caesar in his last moments, as he noticed his adopted son Brutus among his murderers.
Events are the teacher of stupid persons. Stupid people learn by experience, bright people calculate what to do.

latin: Ex abundancia cordis, enim os loquitor.
translation: From the abundance of the heart the mouth speaks.
Without preparation.
From the chair. With authority (without argumentation).
By moral (not legal) obligation.
From the library (of).
For the sake of example.
Each man (is) the maker of his own fortune.
Deeds, not words!

latin: Falsus in uno, falsus in omnibus.
translation: False in one thing, false in all.
Rumor flies (travels fast).
Hunger is the best cook.

latin: Felix qui potuit rerum cognescere causas.
translation: Happy is the one who can learn the causes/reasons of things.
Rush slowly! Do not hasten!
Let justice be done, even though the heavens collapse.
It rocks but does not sink. It is tossed by the waves but it does not sink.

latin: Forsan et haec olim meminisse iuvabit.
translation: Perhaps one day it shall be good to remember even these (tragic) things.
Fortune aids the brave.
By endurance we conquer.

latin: Fortiter in re, suaviter in modo.
translation: Resolutely in deed, gentle in manner.

latin: Fortuna amicos parat, inopia amicos probat.
translation: Fortune is preparing friends, scarcity is in testing them.
It is glorious to forget injustice.

latin: Gutta cavat lapidem (non vi, sed saepe cadendo).
translation: The water drop drills stone (not by the force, but by falling often). Endurance can overcome an obstacle even without force.
Hannibal is at the door! The enemy/danger is at the door! Exclamation of frightened citizens of Rome as enemy army under Hannibal approached the city of Rome.
Here's Rhodus, here jump! Show your skills now! In Aesop's fable, the answer to the boaster who was boasting about a long jump he made on Rhodes.
History is the tutor of life.

latin: Hoc natura est insitum, ut quem timueris, hunc semper oderis.
translation: It's an innate thing to always hate the one we've learnt to fear.
While men teach they learn.
Man is a wolf to man.

latin: Homo sum, humani nihil a me alienum puto.
translation: I am human, therefore nothing human is strange to me.
Exactly the same.

latin: Idem velle atque idem nolle, ea demum firma amicitia est.
translation: Desiring the same things and disliking the same things, that is what true friendship is.
That is (to say).
Ignorance of the law is no excuse. A legal principle whereby ignorance of a law does not allow one to escape liability.
To rule yourself is the ultimate power.
In the tail (of the scorpion) is the poison. Look beyond the obvious in considering possible danger.
In (time of) doubt, in favor of the accused. If there is a doubt about guilt, the judgement has to be in favour of the accused.
While the crime is blazing. Caught in the act.
In the blink of an eye.
In the midst of things.
Virtue stands in the middle. Virtue is in the moderate, not the extreme position.
In memory (of).
The truth is in wine. A drunk person tells the truth.
In time of war, laws are silent.
In time of war, muses (arts) are silent.
Among blind people the squinting one rules.
The right of first night. Belongs to the feudal period when the knights supposedly had the right to be the first to have sexual intercourse with the peasants' newly married (virgin) wives.
Error of the pen.
Error of the tongue.
Error of the memory.
Salute to the reader.
Laws without morals are useless (in vain).
Wolf in the story. Speaking of the devil.

latin: Malum quidem nullum esse sine aliquo bono.
translation: There is, to be sure, no evil without something good.
One hand washes the other. A favor for a favor.
Pearls before swine. To give something valuable to someone not respecting it.
By my guilt.
The physician heals, nature convalesces.
A sound mind in a sound body. Both physical and mental health are necessary.
The world wants to be deceived, so let it be deceived!
Nature is the greatest in the smallest things.
To sail is necessary.
Not even Jupiter (supreme God) can please everyone.

Do not judge higher than the sandal. One should judge only matters that one knows something about. Apelles, Greek painter, would put his pictures where the public could see them and then stand out of sight so he could listen to their comments. A shoemaker once faulted the painter for a sandal with one loop too few, which Apelles corrected. The shoemaker, emboldened by this acceptance of his views, then criticised the subject's leg. According to Pliny Apelles replied to this that the shoemaker should not judge beyond his sandals.
Nobody is blessed before his death. We never know what is future has in store for us.
No man lies freely. A person with no reason to lie is telling the truth.
No one is a judge in his own case. No matter how objective one may be in judging others, one can never keep the same objectivity towards oneself.
No one strikes me with impunity.
No one is considered a prophet in his hometown/homeland.
No man dances sober.
No one is without fault.
Do not let the bastards grind (wear) you down.
Nothing new under the sun.
Nothing but the best is good enough.
I will harm. Medical expression for remedies with no medical effect, which worsen one's medical condition only because one believes they do.
Whether willing or not.

Do not upset my circles! Supposedly said by Archimedes in deep thoughts over geometrical shapes drawn in the sand at the moment a soldier of the conquering Roman army broke into his house and slew him.
The name is the sign.
Not twice for the same thing. No man can be tried a second time on the same charge.

latin: Non omne quod nitet aurum est.
translation: Not everything that shines is gold. Not everything that looks beautiful is good.
Nothing above that!
We learn not for school but for life.
It does not follow. Incorrect statement.

latin: Non uno die Roma aedificata est.
translation: Rome was not built in one day (either).
Know thyself.
Observe carefully.
There is no avarice without penalty.
Not a day without a line. Do something every day!
Nothing in words. Nothing by mere (words of) authority. See for yourself.

latin: Nullum crimen sine lege, nulla poena sine lege.
translation: No crime and no punishment without a (pre-existing) law.

latin: Nullum magnum ingenium sine mixtura dementiae fuit.
translation: There is no great spirit (genius) without a shred of insanity.
Opportunity is offered with difficulty, lost with ease.
Opportunity makes a thief.
All that is mine, I carry with me. My wisdom is my greatest wealth.
Everything is pure to pure ones.
All the best things are rare.
Love conquers all.
Agreements should be obeyed.
Bread and circuses. Food and games to keep people happy.

latin: Parva scintilla saepe magnam flamam excitat.
translation: A small spark often initiates a large flame.

Money has no smell. Money doesn't stink. With the aim of replenishing depleted state funds, Vespasianus introduced, among other things, a new tax on public lavatories.  When objected to by his son Titus, Vespasianus held a coin collected under that tax law to his son's nose and asked him if it smelled.
Punishment comes limping. Retribution comes slowly, but surely.

latin: Per aspera ad astra. (Per ardua ad astra.)
translation: Through thorns to the stars.  (Through struggle to the success.)
By/of itself.
An unwelcome person.
A beard doesn't make a philosopher.
I will please. Medical expression for remedies with no medical effect, which improve one's medical condition only because one believes they do.
After darkness, light.
After text.  (Footnote at the end of a letter.)

latin: Potius sero quam nunquam. Titus Livius
translation: Better late than never.
First, do no harm.
First among equals.
What hurts, often instructs. One learns by bitter/adverse experience.

latin: Quam bene vivas refert, non quam diu.
translation: How well you live makes a difference, not how long.

latin: Qui me amat, amet et canem meum.
translation: Who loves me, loves my dog too.
He who has much desires more.

latin: Qui pro innocente dicit, satis est eloquens.
translation: He who speaks for the innocent is eloquent enough.
Something for something. A reciprocal exchange, something given in compensation, esp. an advantage.

latin: Quidquid agis, prudenter agas et respice finem!
translation: Whatever you do, do cautiously, and look to the end.
Who watches the watchmen?
Where are you going (Lord)? Supposedly said by Apostle Peter to Jesus Christ, whom he encountered when fleeing Rome.

That which is postponed is not dropped. The inevitable is yet to happen. Said by Sir Thomas More, when a false accusation against him made by a new chancellor was at first dropped.   Later he was executed.
What was to be demonstrated. Text in the end of demonstration.

latin: Quod licet Iovi non licet bovi.
translation: What Jupiter (supreme God) is allowed to do, cattle (people) are not.
What nourishes me, destroys me
What is natural cannot be bad.

latin: Quod non est in actis, non est in mundo.
translation: What is not kept in records does not exist. From the Roman Law.

latin: Quos Deus vult perdere, prius dementat.
translation: Those whom God wills to destroy he first deprives of their senses.
As many men as many opinions.
Repeating is the mother of learning.
Rest in Peace.

latin: Scientia non habet inimicum nisi ignorantem.
translation: Science has no enemies but the ignorant.
I know that I know nothing. Certain knowledge cannot be obtained.

latin: Scire tuum nihil est, nisi te scire hoc sciat alter.
translation: Your knowledge is nothing when no one else knows that you know it.
Whoever desires is always poor.
The bones for those who come late. Those who come late get the leavings.

latin: Si Deus pro nobis quis contra nos.
translation: If God is with us who is against us.

latin: Si fractum non sit, noli id reficere.
translation: If it isn't broken, don't fix it.
If you are wise, be a bee.
If you had kept your silence, you would have stayed a philosopher.
If you want peace, prepare for war. Peace is least threatened, when the state is prepared for defence.
Thus you shall go to the stars. Thus is immortality gained.
Thus passes the glory of the world. The world's glory is transitory.
Simplicity is the feature of truth.
Without a date. Without a date limit.  Unknown period of time.
Without the sun I'm silent.
The sun shines upon all.
The present state of affairs.
Infinite is the number of fools.
Highest law, greatest injustice.
A clean slate. Person that knows nothing.

latin: Tempora mutantur, et nos mutamur in illis.
translation: Times are changing, and we are changing within them.
Time flies.
An unknown land. Unexplored by mankind.

I fear the Greeks, even when they bring gifts. Do not trust to the kindness of a foe. According to Vergil, these were the last words of prophet Laocoon, warning the Trojans about the present of the Trojan Horse.  Laocoon was strangled by the monster from the sea and his warning was disregarded.
Where one feels good, there is one's homeland.
Where is unity, there is the victory.
The weak (minor) capitulates before the strong (major).
One Swallow does not make Summer.
To be loved, love!
A grape changes color in seeing another grape. A bad/good friend makes you a bad/good person.
Come with me. A constant companion.

Woe to the vanquished! After he defeated the Romans in 390 BC, the leader of the Gauls Brennus agreed that for payment of a thousand pounds of gold, he would withdraw his army.  According to Livy, when the Romans complained that sets of weights for weighing out the gold brought by Gauls were too heavy, one of the Gaulish warriors tossed his sword into the balance pan, uttering these words.
Diversity is delightful.
I came, I saw, I conquered. Easy accomplishment.
Words move people, examples draw/compel them. Deeds, not words, give the example.
Words fly away, writings remain.
A word to the wise is sufficient. Enough said.
Truth never perishes.
Virtue is the highest reward.
The true and the made are interchangeable. One can know with certainty only what one have created oneself.
Higher force.
Fortune, not wisdom, rules lives.
Learn to live; Remember death.
The voice of the people is the voice of God. Public opinion is obligatory.
Every (hour) wounds, the last kills.
A fox may change its hair, not its tricks. People change behaviour but not their aims.
